attributes.stem:
A 75-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with his son for review of visual hallucinations. His son reports that over the past year his father has gone through periods where he seems confused, agitated, and inattentive but then returns to being himself. Over the past six months, he has developed a mild resting tremor. His mood has been low and he has shown a lack of interest in his daily activities. He has a history of hypertension and poorly controlled type 2 diabetes, for which he takes metformin and amlodipine.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 72/min, blood pressure 150/90 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. When walking into the room, he demonstrates a slightly shuffling gait. His cranial nerve examination is unremarkable. On passive flexion and extension, his arms feel mildly rigid. His power, reflexes, and sensation are normal on examination bilaterally. His mini mental state examination score is 21/30. He has difficulty with language tasks and shows some disinhibited behaviour during the consultation.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Major depressive disorder with psychotic features
attributes.explanation:
This patient likely has Lewy body dementia (LBD). The diagnosis of LBD is made with the presence of at least two of the following core features: visual hallucinations, fluctuating cognition, and parkinsonism. This patient exhibits all three: he has visual hallucinations, periods of confusion and inattention that fluctuate, and mild parkinsonian features (resting tremor, slightly shuffling gait, and mild rigidity). Additionally, REM sleep behaviour disorder is common in LBD, though not mentioned in this case. The cognitive impairment (MMSE 21/30) and low mood are also consistent with LBD.

Parkinson's disease (PD) can present with similar motor symptoms, but cognitive symptoms typically develop more than one year after the onset of motor symptoms. In this case, the cognitive symptoms and hallucinations appeared before or concurrently with the motor symptoms, which is more consistent with LBD than PD.

Vascular dementia is characterized by cognitive decline due to cerebrovascular disease. While it can cause mild parkinsonian features and cognitive fluctuations, it typically does not present with visual hallucinations. The patient's history of hypertension and diabetes does increase his risk for vascular dementia, but the presence of visual hallucinations makes LBD more likely.

Frontotemporal dementia typically presents with changes in behavior, personality, and language rather than memory. The patient's difficulty with language tasks and disinhibited behaviour could suggest frontotemporal dementia. However, visual hallucinations are not characteristic, and the age of onset is typically younger, often between 45-65 years old.

Major depressive disorder with psychotic features could explain the low mood and lack of interest, and potentially the visual hallucinations. However, it would not account for the mild parkinsonian features or the fluctuating cognition. Additionally, psychotic symptoms in depression are more commonly auditory hallucinations or delusions rather than visual hallucinations.

In summary, while the patient has features that could suggest the alternative diagnoses, the combination of visual hallucinations, fluctuating cognition, and mild parkinsonian features strongly suggests Lewy body dementia as the most likely diagnosis in this case.
learningPoints: Lewy Body Dementia classically presents with visual hallucinations, fluctuating cognition, and parkinsonism, often accompanied by REM sleep behaviour disorder.
Cognitive symptoms typically present after motor symptoms in Parkinson's disease, whereas they often occur concurrently or earlier in Lewy Body Dementia.
references: McKeith IG, Boeve BF, Dickson DW, Halliday G, Taylor JP, Weintraub D et al. Diagnosis and management of dementia with Lewy bodies: Fourth consensus report of the DLB Consortium. Neurology. 2017;89(1):88-100. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5496518/

Zupancic M, Mahajan A, Handa K. Dementia with Lewy bodies: diagnosis and management for primary care providers. Prim Care Companion CNS Disord. 2011;13(5). Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22295275/

Galvin JE. Improving the clinical detection of lewy body dementia with the lewy body composite risk score. Alzheimers Dement (Amst). 2015;1(3):316-24. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26405688/

Fischer C, Bozanovic R, Atkins JH, et al. Treatment of delusions in dementia with Lewy bodies: response to pharmacotherapy. Dement Geriatr Cogn Disord. 2007;23(5):307-11. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17356274/

O'Brien JT, Holmes C, Jones M, et al. Clinical practice with anti-dementia drugs: a revised (third) consensus statement from the British Association for Psychopharmacology. J Psychopharmacol. 2017;31(2):147-68. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28103749/

Rolinski M, Fox C, Maidment I, et al. Cholinesterase inhibitors for dementia with Lewy bodies, Parkinson's disease dementia and cognitive impairment in Parkinson's disease. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2012;(3):CD006504. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22419314/

McKeith I, Del-Ser T, Spano P, et al. Efficacy of rivastigmine in dementia with Lewy bodies: a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled international study. Lancet. 2000;356(9247):2031-6. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11145488/

Aarsland D, Mosimann UP, McKeith IG. Role of cholinesterase inhibitors in Parkinson's disease and dementia with Lewy bodies. J Geriatr Psychiatry Neurol. 2004;17(3):164-71. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15312280/

Emre M, Tsolaki M, Bonuccelli U, et al. Memantine for patients with Parkinson's disease dementia or dementia with Lewy bodies: a randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial. Lancet Neurol. 2010;9(10):969-77. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20729148/

Drach LM. Drug treatment of dementia with Lewy bodies and Parkinson's disease dementia: common features and differences [in German]. Med Monatsschr Pharm. 2011;34(2):47-52. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21428015/
